title: Physical & Chemical Change
subtitle: Spotting when a new substance has been made
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: Conduct investigations to predict and identify the products of a range of reactions
Investigate the indicators of chemical change
Model the rearrangement of atoms during chemical reactions
lessonIntent: Students can classify a change as physical or chemical and justify the call using observable indicators of chemical change.
examSkill: Identify multiple indicators of chemical change in a described scenario and explain why a temperature change alone is insufficient evidence.
productionNotes: Mirrors site lesson `subjects/chemistry/year11/module3/lesson01.html`. Real-world anchor: fireworks. Worked example: Zn + H2SO4 (verbatim from site). Misconception: NH4NO3 dissolving (temperature trap). Conservation of mass (Lavoisier 1770s) carried as the underlying mechanism. unitCancel used loosely for conservation of atoms.

scenes.caption: Physical and Chemical Change — Year 11 Chemistry, Module 3.

voiceover.text: Physical and Chemical Change. Year eleven Chemistry, Module three.

scenes.heading: What makes a change "chemical"?
scenes.body: Ice melts. Iron rusts. Sugar dissolves. A match burns. All four look like change — but only two of them have actually made something new. Before any rule arrives, the question is: how would you tell which is which?

bullets.text: Ice melts · Iron rusts · Sugar dissolves · Match burns.
But only two have actually made something new.
Your job: pick which is which.
Test: was a new substance made?

scenes.callout: The test is never about appearance — it's about new substances.
scenes.caption: Some changes rearrange the same matter. Others build something new.

voiceover.text: Let's start with a question, because this whole lesson hinges on it. Ice melts, iron rusts, sugar dissolves, and a match burns. All four of those look like change, but only two of them have actually made something new. And on every short-answer question that asks you to classify a change, your job is to know exactly which is which. The test, as you'll see in a moment, is never about how the change looks. It's always about whether a new substance has been formed.

scenes.heading: Same substance or new substance?
scenes.body: Every change you classify in HSC chemistry comes down to one question: did atoms get rearranged into a different substance? If yes, it's chemical. If no — if you've only changed the state or the form — it's physical.

bullets.text: Physical change: same substance, different state or form.
Chemical change: a new substance with different properties.
Atoms aren't created or destroyed — they're rearranged (Lavoisier, 1770s).
Ice → water → steam: all H₂O. That's physical.

scenes.callout: Ask first: did atoms get rearranged?
scenes.caption: Physical change keeps the substance. Chemical change builds a new one.

voiceover.text: So here's the actual test. Every change you'll classify in chemistry comes down to one question, did atoms get rearranged into a different substance? If yes, it's chemical, and if no, it's physical. Take ice melting. It's the same H two O before and after, just in a different state, so that's physical. Now take iron rusting. The metal becomes iron oxide, a different compound entirely with different properties, and that's chemical. In both cases the atoms themselves aren't created or destroyed, which is the law of conservation of mass, from Lavoisier in the seventeen seventies. They're simply rearranged.

scenes.heading: Five indicators of chemical change
scenes.body: You can't always see bonds breaking. But chemical reactions leave observable clues. NSW HSC requires you to recognise five.

bullets.text: Colour change — copper turns blue-green when it tarnishes.
Gas evolved — bubbles forming where there was no gas before.
Precipitate — an insoluble solid appearing in a clear solution.
Temperature change — the reaction warms up or cools down.
Solid disappearing — a reactant consumed by the reaction.

scenes.callout: Name the indicator AND the new substance for full marks.
scenes.caption: Five observable clues that something new has been made.

voiceover.text: Now, the problem is — you can't actually watch atoms rearranging. But chemical reactions leave observable clues, and HSC wants you to recognise five of them. Colour change, like copper tarnishing blue-green. Gas evolved — bubbles forming where there was no gas before. Precipitate — an insoluble solid appearing in a clear solution. Temperature change — the reaction warms up or cools down. And solid disappearing — a reactant being consumed. The HSC trick: in your answer, name the indicator AND name the new substance. That's how you score full marks instead of half.

scenes.heading: Atoms are reshuffled, not made
scenes.body: Hydrogen plus oxygen forms water: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. Same four hydrogens, same two oxygens — they just bond differently. Total mass before equals total mass after. That's the law of conservation of mass.
scenes.callout: Balance equations by changing coefficients — never subscripts.
scenes.caption: Conservation of mass: atoms are rearranged, not created or destroyed.

voiceover.text: Quick aside on the mechanism, because this matters when you start balancing equations. Think of atoms like Lego bricks. Two H two plus O two gives two H two O — same bricks, snapped together in a new shape. Four hydrogens before, four hydrogens after. Two oxygens before, two oxygens after. Total mass on the left equals total mass on the right. That's conservation of mass at work. But unlike Lego, those new bonds store and release energy. So when you balance an equation, change the coefficient in front of a formula. Never the subscript inside it. Subscripts change what the substance even is.

scenes.heading: Multi-indicator analysis
scenes.question: A student adds a piece of zinc metal to a test tube of dilute sulfuric acid. They observe: the zinc gradually disappears, bubbles form vigorously, and the test tube becomes warm. Identify all indicators of chemical change present, and write the balanced equation for the reaction.
scenes.coachNote: Target: list every indicator AND name the new substance. Multiple indicators is a stronger answer than one.
steps: Known: Zn(s) added to dilute H₂SO₄(aq); zinc disappears; bubbles form; tube warms.
Find: indicators present + balanced equation for the new substances formed.
Indicator 1 — solid disappearing: zinc is being consumed.
Indicator 2 — gas evolved: bubbles are H₂(g), confirmed by squeaky-pop test.
Indicator 3 — temperature increase: reaction is exothermic.
Substitute and balance: Zn(s) + H₂SO₄(aq) → ZnSO₄(aq) + H₂(g) — already balanced.
Answer = 3 indicators present. Products: ZnSO₄(aq) and H₂(g).
scenes.caption: Three indicators here. Products: zinc sulfate and hydrogen gas.

voiceover.text: Right, let's apply all of that to a worked example. A student drops a piece of zinc metal into dilute sulfuric acid. They see the zinc disappearing, vigorous bubbles forming, and the tube warming up. Target: list every indicator of chemical change AND name the new substance. Solid disappearing — that's the zinc being consumed. Gas evolved — those bubbles are hydrogen, confirmed by the squeaky-pop test. Temperature increase — the reaction is exothermic. Three indicators all at once, which is a much stronger argument than just one. The balanced equation: zinc plus sulfuric acid gives zinc sulfate in solution plus hydrogen gas. Products with totally different properties from zinc and acid — definitively chemical.

scenes.heading: Temperature change alone is not enough
scenes.body: Students often think: "It got cold — therefore a chemical reaction happened." Wrong instinct. Dissolving ammonium nitrate in water is endothermic — the beaker gets cold — but no new substance has formed. The NH₄⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions are the same chemicals, just dispersed in water. That's a physical change.
scenes.callout: Look for a NEW substance — or two indicators together — not temperature alone.
scenes.caption: A cold pack is endothermic but physical — no new substance is formed.

voiceover.text: Now, this is where most students lose marks — so pay attention. There's a trap waiting in the temperature indicator. Students often think: it got cold, therefore a chemical reaction. Wrong instinct. Take dissolving ammonium nitrate in water — it's endothermic, the beaker actually gets cold enough to be sold as an instant cold pack. But no new substance has formed. The ammonium ions and nitrate ions are the exact same chemicals before and after, just dispersed in water. That's physical, not chemical. So the fix: temperature change is one indicator, not the test. Look for a new substance — or for two indicators together — before you commit to chemical.

scenes.heading: Fireworks: the perfect demonstration
scenes.body: A firework demonstrates four indicators at once — colour, gas, temperature, and consumption of the metal salt. The original strontium or copper compound is destroyed and cannot be recovered. Definitively chemical.
scenes.caption: Fireworks: colour, gas, heat, and a new substance — all at once.

voiceover.text: And speaking of multiple indicators happening together — fireworks are the textbook example. Four indicators happen simultaneously: colour change from the metal salt burning, gas evolved as the propellant ignites, temperature rising from combustion, and the original strontium or copper compound being consumed entirely. You can't get that metal salt back. That's chemical change in real time, and it's a perfect anchor for short-answer questions.

scenes.heading: Your turn
scenes.question: A blue solution of copper sulfate has magnesium ribbon dropped in. The solution turns colourless and a copper-coloured solid forms on the magnesium. List the indicators of chemical change present.
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause: list every indicator before the answer plays.
answerSteps: Indicator 1 — colour change: blue to colourless (Cu²⁺ ions consumed).
Indicator 2 — precipitate: copper-coloured solid (Cu metal) appearing.
Indicator 3 — solid disappearing: magnesium being consumed.
Answer: three indicators. Magnesium has displaced copper — chemical change confirmed.
scenes.caption: Three indicators. Magnesium displaces copper from solution.

voiceover.text: Time for you to try one. A blue copper sulfate solution has magnesium ribbon dropped in. The solution goes colourless, and a copper-coloured solid appears on the magnesium. List every indicator of chemical change. Pause now, work it out, then come back for the answer. Right — three indicators. Colour change: blue to colourless as the copper-two-plus ions are consumed. Precipitate: the copper-coloured solid is actually solid copper metal forming on the ribbon. And solid disappearing: the magnesium is being eaten away. Magnesium has displaced copper from solution — definitively chemical.

scenes.heading: Summary
scenes.finalPrompt: Have new substances with different properties been formed?
points: Chemical change: a new substance with different properties is formed.
Five indicators: colour change, gas evolved, precipitate, temperature change, solid disappearing.
Multiple indicators is a stronger answer than one — name them and name the new substance.
Temperature change alone is not proof — NH₄NO₃ dissolving is cold but physical.
Conservation of mass: atoms are rearranged, never created or destroyed.
scenes.caption: Five indicators, one defining test, and one decision rule.

voiceover.text: Right, before we wrap, here's the lesson distilled to five takeaways you'll actually use. One: chemical change means a new substance with different properties has formed. Two: the five HSC indicators — colour change, gas evolved, precipitate, temperature change, and solid disappearing. Three: multiple indicators together is always a stronger argument than one, and you score better marks naming the new substance, not just the indicator. Four: temperature change alone isn't enough — ammonium nitrate dissolving is cold but physical. Five: the mechanism behind all of this is conservation of mass — atoms are rearranged, never created or destroyed. So your decision rule on the day: have new substances with different properties been formed?
